title: A subtle reminder to EEZ negotiators
primary_topics: Supreme Court oversight of foreign agreements
sub_topics: Tripartite Agreement for Joint Marine Seismic Undertaking (JMSU)
Article XII Section 2 — full control and supervision of the State
PH-US Mutual Defense Treaty as backstop
Marcos Jr. 'find other ways' November 2022 statement

stances.claim: The Supreme Court's voiding of the JMSU is a subtle reminder to negotiators to comply rigorously with two sacred constitutional safeguards: state full control and supervision over natural resources, and 60% Filipino ownership of qualifying corporations.
stances.rhetorical_move: doctrinal restatement + reframing of decision as governance reminder

stances.claim: The Court went beyond the literal 'pre-exploration activities' label and looked at parties' intent — finding the JMSU was 'tantamount to exploration' — which signals to negotiators that euphemistic labels will not shield non-compliant agreements.

The Philippine panel is powerless to go beyond the February 2022 draft MOU; the simple provision that 'oil and gas operations shall be subject to the laws of the Philippines' satisfies the constitutional safeguards — China should now agree to finalize.

Continued Chinese procrastination 'smacks of just dribbling the ball and refusing to take a shot' — if it continues, the President should not be faulted for abandoning government-to-government talks and turning to the United States, whose mutual defense treaty could be invoked to deploy US forces in the EEZ.
stances.rhetorical_move: basketball metaphor (culturally resonant) + escalation pathway to US alliance

cases: JMSU constitutionality case (12-2-1 decision penned by Gaerlan)

signature_phrases: These safeguards are sacred and must be complied with strictly and faithfully
dribbling the ball and refusing to take a shot
register_markers: ALL CAPS sentence openings as structural device
Catholic eulogy closer ('rest forever in the peace, joy, and love of our Father in heaven')
decision_framework_signals: applies intent-over-form interpretive lens to test compliance with constitutional safeguards
frames a Supreme Court decision as a strategic signaling device to foreign counterparties
uses escalation-pathway analysis (China-PH negotiation → US-PH alliance backstop) for resource disputes
cross_references: As pointed out in my column on Dec. 5, 2022 (titled 'Chinese cooperation to extract our EEZ wealth')
notable_anecdotes: Eddie Yap (Edsa Busway advocate) texting him 'why did it take 15 years to decide a constitutional issue which is pretty straightforward even to lay persons?'
